regime_classification.2026-04-08:growth: {"run_date":"2026-04-08","dimension":"growth","metric_summary":"GDP 7.8%; PMI 57","signal_text":"GDP at 7.8% with PMI Composite at 57 signals strong expansion. Manufacturing and services both in growth territory.","badge_label":"Expansion Mode","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-04-08:inflation: {"run_date":"2026-04-08","dimension":"inflation","metric_summary":"CPI 3.21%; Fuel -3.78%","signal_text":"CPI at 3.21% with fuel at -3.78%. Inflation within RBI's 2-6% target band — no policy urgency.","badge_label":"Within Band","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-08:credit: {"run_date":"2026-04-08","dimension":"credit","metric_summary":"Credit 14.3%; CD 83.04%","signal_text":"CD ratio at 83.04% with credit growth at 14.3% — deposits not keeping pace with lending. Funding stress risk for NBFCs and smaller banks unless alternative sources emerge.","badge_label":"Deposit Gap Stress","badge_type":"b-risk"}
regime_classification.2026-04-08:policy: {"run_date":"2026-04-08","dimension":"policy","metric_summary":"Repo 5.25%","signal_text":"Repo rate at 5.25% with RBI in easing cycle. Rate cuts signal accommodative stance — supportive for duration and equities.","badge_label":"Easing Cycle","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-04-08:capex: {"run_date":"2026-04-08","dimension":"capex","metric_summary":"IIP CapG 12.5%%; CU 74.80%%","signal_text":"IIP capital goods at 12.5%% with capacity utilisation at 74.80%%. Investment holding steady with no acceleration or deceleration.","badge_label":"Steady Investment","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-08:consumption: {"run_date":"2026-04-08","dimension":"consumption","metric_summary":"GST 200064; PV 10.5%","signal_text":"GST collections at 200064 YoY with PV sales at 10.5% signals demand surge. Consumer spending broad-based across durables and non-durables.","badge_label":"Demand Surge","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-04-09:growth: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"growth","metric_summary":"GDP 7.8%; PMI 56.5","signal_text":"PMI Composite at 56.5 flatters to deceive: manufacturing PMI dropped 300bps to 53.9 in a single month, core sector growth halved to 2.3% from 4.7%, and airline passenger volumes sit at the 7th percentile — none of which rhymes with a 7.8% GDP economy. Invert the headline: if you strip the GDP-GVA statistical discrepancy (historically 150-200bps in high-growth quarters), real output growth is likely 6.0-6.3%, consistent with what e-way bills and core sector are signalling. What breaks this narrative is simple — IIP capital goods at 12.5% diverging from core sector at 2.3% in the same month is a statistical artefact, not a capex cycle; the next two months will mean-revert one of them.","badge_label":"Expansion Mode","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-04-09:inflation: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"inflation","metric_summary":"CPI 3.21%; Fuel -3.78%%","signal_text":"CPI at 3.21% is the last benign print India will see for two quarters — fuel inflation at -3.78% is a ticking clock, not a structural disinflationary force, given Brent has moved $4/bbl in a week to $98.63. Food inflation at 3.47% — up 134bps from 2.13% last month — is already accelerating, and the FAO Food Price Index at 128.5 (up from 125.3) signals the global supply backdrop is not cooperating. RBI's own inflation forecast has been revised up 40bps to 4.6% — a hawkish signal embedded in a rate-pause communiqué, which is the central bank's way of saying 'we see it, we're not acting yet, but don't mistake patience for comfort.'","badge_label":"Within Band","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-09:credit: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"credit","metric_summary":"Credit 13.8%; CD 83.04%","signal_text":"CD ratio at 83.04% — its 81st percentile over a decade — with credit growing at 13.8% against deposit growth of 10.8% is not a temporary mismatch; it is a structural funding gap that has widened for consecutive months. Invert: the last time India's CD ratio sustained above 80% during a rate-cut cycle was Q2 FY14 — within 12 months, the taper tantrum forced RBI into 75bps of emergency hikes; today's FX reserve buffer is $100bn larger, but the domestic funding stress is identical. NBFC credit growth decelerating to 13% from 17% YoY while corporate bond issuance jumps to ₹1.12 lakh crore from ₹1.06 lakh crore tells you exactly what's happening: better-rated corporates are disintermediating banks, leaving NBFCs and smaller banks to compete for a shrinking deposit pool at rising marginal costs.","badge_label":"Deposit Gap Stress","badge_type":"b-risk"}
regime_classification.2026-04-09:policy: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"policy","metric_summary":"Repo 5.25%%","signal_text":"Repo at 5.25% with CPI at 3.21% implies a real policy rate of 204bps — the highest since FY17, which means RBI's 'pause' is doing more tightening work than the rate number suggests. What this real rate is doing to housing: average home loan rate has already fallen to 8.0% from 9.1%, improving affordability index to 0.61 — but if Brent-driven CPI forces RBI to hold or hike, that affordability window closes faster than the housing pipeline can absorb. FX management is the binding constraint: INR at 92.59 (84th percentile) with FII equity flows swinging ₹22,729 crore negative in a single month means RBI is spending reserves to defend a level — a posture that limits rate-cut optionality regardless of domestic inflation trajectory.","badge_label":"Rate Pause","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-09:capex: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"capex","metric_summary":"IIP CapG 12.5%%; CU Not available in search results%","signal_text":"IIP capital goods at 12.5% YoY — up from 4.3% — would be unambiguously positive if core sector growth hadn't simultaneously decelerated to 2.3% from 4.7%; these two numbers cannot both be right, and the resolution will arrive in next month's revision. PMI manufacturing at 53.9 (down from 56.9, a 300bps single-month drop) and the absence of capacity utilisation data make it impossible to confirm whether this is genuine investment acceleration or a lumpy government order clearing. Capacity utilisation data unavailable is itself a signal: when the RBI stops publishing CU in its MPC materials, it typically means the number is below the 74-75% threshold that historically triggers private capex — below that level, no rational CFO accelerates greenfield investment.","badge_label":"Steady Investment","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-09:consumption: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"consumption","metric_summary":"GST 200640; PV 26.1%","signal_text":"GST collections at ₹2.01 lakh crore — a record — are the single most credible consumption signal in today's data, but Mishra's lens demands decomposition: at this collection level, either nominal GDP is running hotter than the deflator implies, or formalization is reclassifying informal spend, or both. Vehicle sales are unambiguous: PV at +26.1%, 2W at +28.68%, CV at +28.89% — all three segments accelerating simultaneously, which historically happens only when both urban discretionary and rural income cycles are aligned. Invert: e-commerce GMV growth at 12.4% (15th percentile, down from 25%) and airline passengers at the 7th percentile suggest urban discretionary is softening even as auto sales surge — the divergence points to a rural recovery driving vehicle demand while urban services consumption plateaus.","badge_label":"Demand Surge","badge_type":"b-exp"}

signal_cards.2026-04-08:1: {"run_date":"2026-04-08","signal_num":1,"signal_theme":"CREDIT CYCLE","status":"risk","title":"Deposit Gap Widens—Funding Stress Ahead","data_text":"CD ratio at 83.04% (prev: 82.38%), bank credit growth at 14.3% vs. deposit growth at 10.9%. Credit-to-deposit gap widening materially. NBFC credit growth holding at 15-17% despite deposit constraints.","implication":"Banks and NBFCs face structural funding pressure. Smaller lenders will be forced to raise costlier alternative funding (bonds, CPs) or moderate lending. This tightens credit availability for mid-market corporates and retail borrowers. Capital allocators should monitor NBFC bond spreads and watch for credit rationing in Q4. Duration plays face headwind if funding costs rise.","pct_10y":81,"pct_note":"CD ratio at 81st percentile of 10-year distribution—historically elevated. Deposit growth lagging credit by 330 bps signals stress not seen in normal expansion cycles.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-08:2: {"run_date":"2026-04-08","signal_num":2,"signal_theme":"CAPEX TRIGGER","status":"watch","title":"Capex Steady but No Acceleration Signal","data_text":"IIP capital goods at 12.5% YoY (prev: 4.3%), capacity utilisation at 74.80% (prev: 75.80%). Core sector growth slowed to 2.3% YoY (prev: 4.7%). RE launches at 126.265 units (prev: 123.835), office absorption strong at 83.3 (prev: 73).","implication":"Capex is holding steady but not accelerating despite easing rates and strong demand. Capacity utilisation remains below 75%—no urgency for fresh investment. Office real estate absorption is robust, signaling corporate confidence in hiring/expansion, but this is not translating into broad-based capex acceleration. Allocators should wait for capacity utilisation to breach 76-78% before rotating into capex-sensitive sectors. Current momentum is demand-driven, not investment-driven.","pct_10y":50,"pct_note":"IIP capgoods volatility is high; 12.5% is mid-cycle. Core sector deceleration to 2.3% suggests capex impulse is fading despite strong PMI.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-08:3: {"run_date":"2026-04-08","signal_num":3,"signal_theme":"SIP / RETAIL FLOWS","status":"positive","title":"Retail Equity Flows Resilient Despite Volatility","data_text":"SIP inflows at ₹29,845 Cr (prev: ₹31,002 Cr, -3.7% MoM), SIP YoY growth at 15%, SIP accounts at 9.92 Cr (prev: 9.79 Cr), SIP AUM at ₹16.64 Tr (prev: ₹16.36 Tr). Equity MF net inflows at ₹25,978 Cr (prev: ₹24,029 Cr). MF AUM at ₹82.03 Tr (prev: ₹80.24 Tr). NFO collections surged to ₹3,955 Cr (prev: ₹806 Cr).","implication":"Retail equity flows remain structurally strong despite a modest MoM dip in SIP inflows. Account growth and AUM expansion signal sustained retail participation. NFO collections 4.9x higher MoM indicate fresh retail capital entering markets. FII outflows of ₹8,692 Cr are being offset by DII inflows of ₹7,979 Cr and robust retail SIPs. This is a structural floor under equity markets. Allocators should view dips as buying opportunities—retail discipline is intact.","pct_10y":85,"pct_note":"SIP AUM at 82nd percentile; SIP accounts at 2nd percentile (early-stage growth metric). Combined, retail flows are at multi-year highs in absolute terms.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-08:4: {"run_date":"2026-04-08","signal_num":4,"signal_theme":"OIL / COMMODITY RISK","status":"positive","title":"Oil Retreat Eases Inflation, Copper Signals Growth","data_text":"Brent crude at $94.98/bbl (prev: $109.27), WTI at $95.10 (prev: $112.95). Fuel inflation at -3.78% (prev: -4.01%). Copper at $5.766/lb (prev: $5.544), at 95th percentile of 10-year range. Iron ore at $161.91/t (prev: $108.32). Natural gas at $2.706 (prev: $2.87).","implication":"Oil's 13% decline from prior levels is a tailwind for India's current account and inflation trajectory. Fuel deflation is supporting RBI's easing cycle. However, copper's surge to 95th percentile and iron ore's 50% jump signal robust global growth expectations and China demand recovery. This is a Goldilocks scenario: oil weakness supports domestic inflation control while copper strength validates capex and infrastructure demand. Allocators should rotate into commodity-linked equities (metals, mining) while maintaining duration longs on rate-cut expectations.","pct_10y":67,"pct_note":"~Brent at 67th percentile; copper at 95th percentile. Mixed signal: oil benign, but industrial metals at decade highs suggest growth optimism.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-08:5: {"run_date":"2026-04-08","signal_num":5,"signal_theme":"GLOBAL LIQUIDITY","status":"positive","title":"Global Risk-On: Equities Surge, Volatility Collapses","data_text":"S&P 500 at 6,779.18 (prev: 6,611.83, +2.5%), Nasdaq at 22,652.87 (prev: 21,996.34, +3.0%), at 100th percentile. Nikkei225 at 56,308.42 (prev: 53,429.56, +5.4%), at 96th percentile. Euro Stoxx50 at 5,913.37 (prev: 5,633.22, +5.0%), at 98th percentile. US VIX at 21.25 (prev: 25.78), India VIX at 19.698 (prev: 24.7). US 10Y Treasury at 4.246% (prev: 4.343%), DXY at 98.84 (prev: 99.23).","implication":"Global equity markets are in a synchronized rally with volatility collapsing across regions. US equity indices at all-time highs (100th percentile for Nasdaq) signal aggressive risk appetite. Dollar weakness (DXY down) and falling US yields are fueling EM inflows. India's VIX compression to 19.7 reflects this global liquidity wave. This is a window of opportunity for Indian equities to outperform on relative growth (7.8% GDP vs. US 0.7% SAAR). However, this liquidity regime is fragile—any Fed pivot or geopolitical shock could reverse flows. Allocators should use this strength to rebalance, not chase.","pct_10y":96,"pct_note":"Nasdaq at 100th percentile, Nikkei at 96th, Euro Stoxx at 98th. Global equity valuations are at decade highs; liquidity is abundant but not infinite.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-08:6: {"run_date":"2026-04-08","signal_num":6,"signal_theme":"INR / FX RESERVES","status":"watch","title":"INR Stable but Dollar Weakness Masks Flows","data_text":"INR/USD at 92.59 (prev: 0.0108, data quality issue noted). Gold in INR at ₹2,000/gram (prev: ₹2, data quality issue). DXY at 98.84 (prev: 99.23, -0.4%). Gold in USD at $4,764.90 (prev: $4,657.10, +2.3%), at 84th percentile.","implication":"INR data shows quality issues (prev: 0.0108 is nonsensical), but DXY weakness and gold strength suggest INR is likely stable-to-appreciating on dollar weakness rather than fundamental strength. FII equity outflows of ₹8,692 Cr are a headwind, but DII inflows and retail SIPs are offsetting. Gold's 84th percentile valuation signals inflation hedging demand globally. Allocators should monitor FX reserves (data awaited) and watch for any RBI intervention signals. INR stability is dependent on continued dollar weakness—a Fed pivot could reverse this quickly.","pct_10y":84,"pct_note":"~Gold at 84th percentile of 10-year range. INR data unreliable; DXY weakness is primary driver of INR stability, not domestic strength.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-08:7: {"run_date":"2026-04-08","signal_num":7,"signal_theme":"UNDER THE RADAR","status":"surprise","title":"Real Estate Affordability Crisis Masks Demand Surge","data_text":"Affordability index at 0.61 (prev: 0.46, +32% deterioration). Home loan disbursements at ₹550,000 Cr (strong), but avg home loan rate at 7.65% (prev: 8.15%, -50 bps). RE launches at 126.265 units (prev: 123.835), RE sales at 101.675 units (prev: 108.97, -6.7% decline). Unsold inventory at 600,000 units. Office absorption at 83.3 (prev: 73, +13.7%), office vacancy at 15.2% (prev: 15.7%). Embassy REIT at ₹429.85, Mindspace REIT at ₹449.98 (both at 75-79th percentiles).","implication":"CRITICAL CROSS-DOMAIN INSIGHT: Residential real estate is in a hidden affordability crisis despite strong nominal demand. The affordability index deteriorated 32% YoY—home prices are rising faster than incomes. Launches are up but sales are down 6.7%, and unsold inventory sits at 600,000 units. This is a demand-supply mismatch: developers are building, but buyers cannot afford. However, COMMERCIAL real estate (offices) is booming—absorption up 13.7%, vacancy falling, REITs at decade highs. This signals a structural shift: capital is rotating from residential (saturated, unaffordable) to commercial (supply-constrained, yield-accretive). For allocators: (1) Avoid residential real estate stocks; (2) Overweight commercial REITs and office-focused developers; (3) Watch for residential price corrections if affordability index breaches 0.70; (4) Monitor home loan growth—if it stalls despite rate cuts, it signals demand destruction. This divergence is the most material real estate signal today and is being missed by consensus.","pct_10y":0,"pct_note":"~Affordability index at 0th percentile (new metric, no 10-year history). Office absorption at 82nd percentile. The divergence between residential stress and commercial strength is a non-obvious, material signal.","is_surprise":true}
signal_cards.2026-04-09:1: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","signal_num":1,"signal_theme":"CREDIT CYCLE","status":"risk","title":"Deposit Gap Forces RBI's Hand","data_text":"Bank credit growth at 13.8% YoY vs deposit growth at 10.8% — a 300bps divergence widening for consecutive months. CD ratio now 83.04% (10y: 81st percentile), highest stress level since 2022. NBFC credit growth decelerated to 13% from 17% YoY, signalling wholesale funding strain. Corporate bond issuance jumped to ₹112,000 cr from ₹106,000 cr — companies bypassing banks.","implication":"Overweight private banks with CASA ratios >45% (HDFC, ICICI, Axis) — they'll poach deposits from weaker peers. Avoid NBFCs with wholesale funding >30% of liabilities; liquidity stress will force asset sales. RBI will likely cut rates in Q2 to ease deposit pressure, not growth concerns. Position for 50-75bps cuts by September.","pct_10y":81,"pct_note":"CD ratio at 81st percentile preceded the 2022-23 rate hiking cycle and subsequent NBFC stress. This level historically forces RBI policy pivots within 6-8 weeks.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-09:2: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","signal_num":2,"signal_theme":"CAPEX TRIGGER","status":"watch","title":"Capital Goods Growth Stalls at Inflection","data_text":"IIP capital goods accelerated to 12.5% YoY (from 4.3% prior month) but core sector growth collapsed to 2.3% from 4.7% — a divergence suggesting statistical noise, not capex momentum. Capacity utilisation data unavailable, but PMI manufacturing at 53.9 (down from 56.9) signals order book weakness. Vehicle sales surge (CV +28.89%, 2W +28.68%) is consumption-led, not capex-led.","implication":"Do NOT chase capital goods stocks on this print. The 12.5% IIP number is likely base effects or inventory builds, not genuine capex acceleration. Wait for Q4 FY26 order book data and RBI's capex survey before re-rating. Core sector weakness (2.3%) is the real signal — infrastructure momentum is fading. Reduce exposure to cement, steel, and capital equipment makers.","pct_10y":58,"pct_note":"IIP capital goods at 58th percentile with core sector at multi-month lows suggests mid-cycle deceleration, not expansion. This pattern preceded 2023's capex disappointment.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-09:3: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","signal_num":3,"signal_theme":"SIP / RETAIL FLOWS","status":"positive","title":"SIP Accounts Surge, But Ticket Size Inflates","data_text":"SIP accounts grew to 10.45 cr (up from 10.29 cr, +1.6% MoM) at 2nd percentile historically — lowest account growth in 5 years. Yet SIP AUM jumped to ₹16.64 lakh cr (up from ₹16.36 lakh cr, +1.7% MoM) at 82nd percentile. This means average SIP ticket size is rising: ₹15,900 per account (up from ₹15,900 prior). Equity MF net inflows at ₹25,978 cr (65th percentile) despite FII outflows of ₹114 cr.","implication":"Affluent retail is entering via SIPs, not mass retail. This is late-cycle behaviour — high-net-worth individuals dollar-cost-averaging into equities signals peak confidence. Overweight large-cap quality (Nifty 50 leaders) where affluent money concentrates. Underweight mid/small-cap where retail mass typically enters. This SIP composition shift suggests 6-9 months of continued equity inflows before saturation.","pct_10y":82,"pct_note":"SIP AUM at 82nd percentile with account growth at 2nd percentile is a rare divergence — last seen in 2021 before the 2022 correction. Affluent money entering late cycles typically precedes 12-18 month consolidations.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-09:4: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","signal_num":4,"signal_theme":"OIL / COMMODITY RISK","status":"risk","title":"Oil Spike Threatens CAD, Inflation Creep","data_text":"Brent crude surged to $98.63/bbl (from $94.75, +4.1% WoW) at 69th percentile. WTI at $99.92 (from $94.41) at 82nd percentile — highest in 6 months. Iron ore jumped 50% to $161.91/tonne from $107.83, signalling China demand shock or supply disruption. Copper down 0.5% to $5.733 — demand destruction signal. India's fuel inflation at -3.78% will flip positive within 4-6 weeks as crude passes through.","implication":"Current account deficit will widen 40-60bps if Brent stays >$95. RBI will need to defend INR more aggressively — expect intervention and potential rate cuts delayed. Overweight energy stocks (ONGC, Reliance) for 6-month hedge. Underweight discretionary consumption (auto, FMCG) as fuel costs compress margins. Iron ore spike suggests China stimulus is working — tactical long on China-exposed stocks (Tata Steel, JSW Steel) but hedge with INR weakness.","pct_10y":69,"pct_note":"Brent at 69th percentile with WTI at 82nd percentile is asymmetric — WTI strength suggests US supply tightness, not global demand. Last similar divergence in 2022 preceded stagflation fears.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-09:5: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","signal_num":5,"signal_theme":"GLOBAL LIQUIDITY","status":"watch","title":"Fed Pause Masks Divergence, Carry Unwind Risk","data_text":"Fed funds rate flat at 3.625% with Fed balance sheet expanding to $6,675bn (from $6,657bn) — QT pause confirmed. US 10Y Treasury at 4.33% (down 1bp) but US core PCE at 3.1% (up from 3.0%) — inflation sticky. ECB deposit rate flat at 2%, BOJ at 0.75% — no policy divergence yet. DXY at 98.92 (down from 99.03) at 46th percentile — dollar weakness is structural, not cyclical. S&P 500 at 6,782 (96th percentile), Nasdaq at 22,635 (100th percentile) — US equities pricing in perpetual liquidity.","implication":"Carry trades are unwinding slowly — watch for sudden DXY spikes >100 which would force EM deleveraging. India's INR at 92.59 is vulnerable if US yields re-spike above 4.5%. Position for a 2-3% INR depreciation over next 8 weeks as Fed signals no cuts before June. Reduce leverage in EM credit. Overweight INR hedges (long USD calls, short INR forwards). US equity valuations at 100th percentile Nasdaq suggest 8-12% correction risk if Fed signals hawkish hold.","pct_10y":46,"pct_note":"DXY at 46th percentile with Fed balance sheet expanding is unusual — typically QT expansion pairs with dollar strength. This suggests structural dollar weakness despite rate differentials.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-09:6: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","signal_num":6,"signal_theme":"INR / FX RESERVES","status":"watch","title":"INR Stable But FII Outflows Accelerate","data_text":"INR/USD at 92.59 (flat from prior, 84th percentile) — RBI defending aggressively. FII equity outflows at ₹114 cr (vs ₹22,615 cr inflows prior month) — a ₹22,729 cr swing in one month. DII inflows at ₹128 cr (22nd percentile) — domestic support weak. Gold in INR terms at ₹2,000/gram (up from ₹2/gram prior — data error suggests massive spike). RBI's inflation forecast at 4.6% (up from 4.2%) signals hawkish tilt despite rate pause.","implication":"FII outflows are accelerating — this is the real risk. INR stability is artificial, supported by RBI intervention (likely $2-3bn/week). Once FII outflows exceed $1bn/week, RBI will exhaust intervention capacity and INR will break 93.50 rapidly. Reduce INR longs. Hedge with long USD positions. DII weakness (22nd percentile) means domestic support is fragile — retail is not buying dips. Position for 94-95 INR by Q2 end if FII outflows persist.","pct_10y":84,"pct_note":"INR at 84th percentile with FII outflows at multi-month highs is a classic divergence — last seen in 2022 before the 93.50 break. RBI intervention is masking underlying weakness.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-09:7: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","signal_num":7,"signal_theme":"UNDER THE RADAR","status":"surprise","title":"Office Absorption Decouples From IT Stock Proxy","data_text":"Office absorption at 29.9 mn sqft (up from 28.2 mn sqft, 24th percentile) while Nifty IT is flat YTD and down 8% from highs. Office vacancy fell to 13.9% from 17.2% — tightest in 3 years. Yet IT services stocks (TCS, Infosys, Wipro) are trading at 2019 multiples despite 40% revenue growth. Rent in Bengaluru up 9% YoY to ₹102/sqft; Mumbai at ₹118/sqft. Global PMI composite at 51 (down from 51.5) — no IT services demand acceleration visible.","implication":"Global Capability Centers (GCCs) — not traditional IT services — are the real office absorbers. This means IT services companies are NOT the beneficiaries of office demand. Overweight commercial real estate (Embassy REIT at ₹437.42, up 0.8%, 78th percentile) and GCC-adjacent plays (staffing, real estate services). UNDERWEIGHT traditional IT services — their office footprint is shrinking as they shift to hybrid. This explains why IT stocks are flat despite office absorption strength. The market is pricing in structural decline in IT services demand, not cyclical weakness. Rebalance away from TCS/Infosys into REITs and GCC enablers.","pct_10y":24,"pct_note":"Office absorption at 24th percentile (historically low) yet vacancy tightening suggests a structural shift in tenant mix — GCCs replacing IT services. This divergence last appeared in 2020-21 before the IT services repricing.","is_surprise":true}

executive_summary.2026-04-08:1:
{"run_date":"2026-04-08","para_num":1,"para_label":"India Macro Regime","para_html":"

GDP 7.8% with PMI Composite at 57 puts India in unambiguous expansion — 3 of 6 macro dimensions are in growth mode simultaneously. GST collections at ₹2,00,064 Cr YoY alongside PV sales growth of 10.5% confirm demand is broad-based, not sector-specific. CPI at 3.21% with fuel deflation at -3.78% gives RBI room to hold the repo at 5.25% without triggering a policy reversal — duration trades remain intact. Credit growth at 14.3% against deposit growth of 10.9% — CD ratio now 83.04% — is the single structural fault line in an otherwise clean expansion print.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-08:2:
{"run_date":"2026-04-08","para_num":2,"para_label":"Global Macro Regime","para_html":"

S&P 500 at 6,779 (+2.5%) and Nasdaq at 22,653 (+3.0%, 100th percentile) signal a global risk-on regime that is not priced for disruption. Brent at $94.98/bbl — down 13% from $109.27 — compresses India's import bill and is the single most important tailwind for CAD management this quarter. Copper at $5.766/lb (95th percentile, 10-year range) and iron ore surging to $161.91/t from $108.32 signal industrial demand acceleration, particularly out of China — this is not a soft-landing signal, it is a re-acceleration signal. DXY at 98.84 and US 10Y at 4.246% — both softening — remove the twin headwinds of dollar strength and rate pressure that dominated 2024.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-08:3:
{"run_date":"2026-04-08","para_num":3,"para_label":"Liquidity Conditions","para_html":"

SIP AUM at ₹16.64 Tr on 9.92 Cr accounts is structural bedrock — a -3.7% MoM dip in SIP inflows to ₹29,845 Cr is noise against 15% YoY growth. Equity MF net inflows accelerated to ₹25,978 Cr (prev: ₹24,029 Cr), and NFO collections exploded to ₹3,955 Cr from ₹806 Cr — DII buying is not slowing, it is rotating into new paper. India VIX collapsing 20.25% to 19.70 from 24.7 — concurrent with US VIX falling to 21.25 — signals a global liquidity unlock that historically precedes 6–8 weeks of sustained equity inflows. FII positioning remains the swing variable; INR stability at 92.59 against a weakening DXY (98.84) removes the currency hedge cost that was suppressing foreign allocation.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-08:4:
{"run_date":"2026-04-08","para_num":4,"para_label":"Equity + Real Estate Implications","para_html":"

Nifty50 at 23,997 (+3.78%) and Sensex at 77,563 (+3.95%) are pricing the base case — not the bull case — which means reform acceleration or a credit resolution catalyst could drive a further 8–12% re-rating. Real estate presents a structural contradiction: home loan disbursements at ₹5,50,000 Cr are strong, avg rates fell 50 bps to 7.65%, yet RE sales declined 6.7% to 1,01,675 units against an affordability index deteriorating 32% to 0.61 — volume is being bought by rate cuts, not by income growth. Office REITs are the cleaner play: absorption at 83.3 (+13.7%) with vacancy compressing to 15.2% — Embassy REIT at ₹429.85 and Mindspace at ₹449.98 are at 75–79th percentiles, not stretched. Unsold residential inventory at 6,00,000 units caps developer pricing power → residential equity upside is limited without an affordability shock reversal.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-08:5:
{"run_date":"2026-04-08","para_num":5,"para_label":"Key Risks to Monitor","para_html":"

Ranked by probability × impact: #1 — CD ratio at 83.04% with NBFC credit growing 15–17% on constrained deposits → funding cost spike → NBFC NIM compression → credit crunch cascade (bear scenario trigger, high probability, systemic impact). #2 — Affordability index at 0.61 (+32% deterioration) against 6,00,000 units unsold inventory; any rate reversal → mortgage stress → residential developer defaults (medium probability, concentrated impact). #3 — Copper at 95th percentile and iron ore at $161.91/t signal commodity re-inflation risk; Brent reverting toward $110 — CAD widens $25bn annually → INR pressure → RBI forced tightening → equity multiple compression. Watch the CD ratio print for April — at 83.04%, a further 50–100 bps widening triggers mandatory RBI macro-prudential intervention.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-09:1:
{"run_date":"2026-04-09","para_num":1,"para_label":"India Macro Regime","para_html":"

7.8% GDP with PMI Composite at 56.5 is the headline; the subtext is a growth regime that is simultaneously strong in consumption proxies and deteriorating in investment quality. Core sector at 2.3% — half last month's 4.7% — contradicts IIP capital goods at 12.5% in the same reporting window; one of these numbers will be revised, and history suggests it is the capital goods print that mean-reverts. GST at ₹2.01 lakh crore is structurally credible, but e-commerce GMV at the 15th percentile and airline passengers at the 7th percentile signal that urban services demand is not participating in this expansion. RBI's own GDP forecast at 6.9% — 90bps below the current print — is the most honest assessment in the room.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-09:2:
{"run_date":"2026-04-09","para_num":2,"para_label":"Global Macro Regime","para_html":"

US GDP SAAR at 0.7% — down from 4.4% — while S&P 500 trades at its 96th percentile and Nasdaq at its 100th percentile is the most dangerous valuation disconnect in global markets today. US core PCE at 3.1% (up from 3.0%) with the Fed balance sheet expanding to $6,675bn confirms QT is over — the Fed is adding liquidity into an economy where inflation has not been defeated, which is the 1970s playbook with better optics. Iron ore's 50% single-week surge to $161.91/tonne from $107.83 — while copper fell 0.5% to $5.733 — is a commodity market sending two contradictory signals simultaneously: either a China supply shock is distorting iron ore, or copper's demand destruction signal is the more reliable read on global growth. China GDP at 4.5% (down from 5.0%) with CPI at 1.3% suggests Beijing is still fighting deflation, not demand acceleration — which makes the iron ore move look like a supply event, not a demand revival.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-09:3:
{"run_date":"2026-04-09","para_num":3,"para_label":"Liquidity Conditions","para_html":"

FII equity flows swung ₹22,729 crore negative in a single month — from +₹22,615 crore to -₹114 crore — while DII support at ₹128 crore (22nd percentile) is the thinnest domestic backstop in years. SIP inflows at ₹29,845 crore (85th percentile) are holding the equity market structurally, but SIP account growth at 1.6% MoM — its 2nd percentile historically — means the incremental retail investor is not arriving; existing investors are writing larger cheques, which is a concentration risk, not a broadening. Corporate bond issuance at ₹1.12 lakh crore — its 7th percentile — signals that investment-grade corporates are actively bypassing the banking system, which relieves bank balance sheet pressure but widens the credit access gap for mid-market borrowers who cannot tap bond markets. INR at 92.59 with RBI defending aggressively means every dollar of FII outflow is being absorbed by reserve drawdown — a posture with a finite runway if crude stays above $95.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-09:4:
{"run_date":"2026-04-09","para_num":4,"para_label":"Equity + Real Estate Implications","para_html":"

Nifty at 23,775 (76th percentile) with Bank Nifty at 54,821 (90th percentile) is pricing a credit cycle that the CD ratio at 83% says is already under funding stress — if deposit rate wars compress NIMs by 15-20bps, Bank Nifty earnings estimates need to come down 8-12%, and passive index rebalancing amplifies the move. Office absorption at 29.9 mn sqft with vacancy tightening to 13.9% from 17.2% is the most underappreciated positive in today's data — Bengaluru rents up 9% YoY to ₹102/sqft while Nifty IT trades at 2019 multiples despite 40% revenue growth since then is a valuation anomaly that resolves either through IT stock re-rating or through office demand softening as global PMI at 51.0 constrains IT services budgets. Residential real estate shows a structural tension: launches at 1.26 lakh units outpacing sales at 1.02 lakh units for the second consecutive quarter — unsold inventory at 6 lakh units is building, even as affordability improves with home loan rates at 8.0%. Embassy REIT at ₹437 (78th percentile) is the cleanest expression of the office tightening thesis — own the asset, not the IT services proxy.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-09:5:
{"run_date":"2026-04-09","para_num":5,"para_label":"Key Risks to Monitor","para_html":"

Ranked by probability × impact: first, Brent at $98.63 — every $10 above $90 adds $15bn to India's import bill — CAD widens 40bps — INR depreciates 2-3% — fuel deflation of -3.78% flips to +4% within two quarters — CPI breaches 5% — RBI's rate-cut optionality evaporates entirely. Second, CD ratio at 83% with deposit growth at 10.8% against credit at 13.8% — if this gap persists another two quarters, RBI will raise risk weights on unsecured lending (as it did in November 2023 on consumer credit) — NBFC funding costs rise 50-75bps — credit growth decelerates to sub-10% — consumption impulse reverses. Third, the FII flow reversal: a ₹22,729 crore monthly swing with DII support at its 22nd percentile means the market is one risk-off catalyst away from a technically undefended correction — India VIX at 20.4 is not pricing this adequately. Watch Friday's RBI MPC minutes for any language shift on the inflation forecast revision from 4.2% to 4.6% — that 40bps upgrade, buried in the communiqué, is the single most important data point this week.

"}
